title: Economic profile: Inverness County, Nova Scotia - Canada.ca
content:
## Economic profile: Inverness County, Nova Scotia
Inverness County Footnote 1 has a population of 20,129, consisting of 24% seniors (65 and older), 61% working-age adults (15 to 64) and 15% children (under 15).
The key sectors of the economy in Inverness County, which account for 52% of the jobs in the region, are
- health care and social assistance
- educational services
There are 8,905 employed residents, of which 5.1% are immigrants. Footnote 2
The economy generates a total of $997 million in household and business incomes, which amounts to $49,546 per resident.

### General age structure – 2021
% children (under 15)
% working-age adults (15 to 64)
% of seniors (65 and older)
In order to support local services such as health care and social programs and maintain a good standard of living for everyone in the region, it is essential to have more working-age adults than seniors. Inverness County has fewer working-age adults than seniors compared to the average in Nova Scotia.
From 2011 to 2021, Inverness County experienced
- a 2.8% increase in population, compared to a 5.2% increase in the province
- a 52% increase in the population’s proportion of seniors, compared to a 33% increase in the province
- a 10% decrease in the population’s proportion of working-age adults, compared to a 6.8% decrease in the province

### Population structure – Changes from 2011 to 2021
Changes in the region
Changes in the province
Proportion of children
Proportion of working-age adults
Proportion of seniors
By 2031, without immigration, Footnote 3 the region could experience Footnote 4
- a 4.7% decrease in population
- a 3.9% decrease in the population’s proportion of working-age adults, which could limit the availability and quality of local services
- a further increase in the population’s proportion of seniors by 21%, potentially changing the demand for local services, including health care and long-term care

### No immigration – Possible changes from 2021 to 2031
Changes in the region
Changes in the province
Proportion of children
Proportion of working-age adults
Proportion of seniors
The region relies heavily on working-age adults to keep local services running smoothly and to maintain a good standard of living and economic growth. Immigrants can help by filling job gaps and bringing new skills and ideas, which keeps the local economy strong.
From 2011 to 2021, Inverness County experienced
- a 6.4% decrease in workforce Footnote 5 size, compared to a 0.6% increase in the province
- an 8.3% increase in the number of immigrant workers, compared to a 49% increase in the province
- a total of 2,260 retirements, Footnote 6 which amounts to 22% of the 2021 workforce, compared to 20% in the province
- a change in the average income per resident from local jobs relative to the province, which is 5.5% lower than the average in Nova Scotia in 2021

By 2031, without immigration, Footnote 8 the region could experience Footnote 9
- a 35% decrease in workforce size
- a 29% decrease in the number of jobs
- an increase in the proportion of seniors in the population from 24% to 29%

## Supporting the community’s French-speaking population
Francophone immigration outside Quebec plays an important role in enhancing the vitality and economic development of Francophone minority communities across the country.
In Inverness County, the Chéticamp region is part of the Welcoming Francophone Communities initiative launched by Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Canada. This initiative supports the economic and socio-cultural integration of French-speaking newcomers.
In total, 11% of Inverness County’s population speaks French, with 2.5% speaking French as a first language and 8.4% as a second language.
Unless otherwise noted, all statistics are from the 2011, 2016 or 2021 editions of the Statistics Canada Census, and all calculations are from the Canadian Centre for Economic Analysis.
